# Questions in Livestream 108

Live Stream #108: The Many Views of Heck

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=717" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:57</a> | Could there be surprising people in heaven? | He said entry into heaven is fundamentally about relation to Christ rather than passing a theology exam, and he left some room for a `second chance` idea for those who never heard the gospel. | He framed heaven as a relationship test centered on accepting Christ, not as an exam in correct theology. He allowed that some who never heard the gospel may still receive a choice by God's grace, though he distinguished that from receiving rewards, and stressed that God's grace is abundant while justice remains real. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=1412" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:32</a> | How can we tell an Egyptian loanword from a shared Afroasiatic inheritance? | He said the best clue is distribution: if a word has deep history in one branch and later enters another, it is likely a loan. | He noted that Proto-Afroasiatic itself is debated, so the practical test is historical distribution within the language families. A term with a long prior history in Egyptian that later appears in Hebrew or other Semitic languages is likely borrowed from Egyptian, while a Semitic term that predates its Egyptian use points the other direction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=1593" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:33</a> | What was he thinking with the stream title jokes, the missing TV, and the Mount Ebal tablet translation? | He treated the first two as jokes, then criticized the Mount Ebal epigraphy as pareidolia and said the team should have reported an honest negative result. | He joked that he did not know what he was thinking with the title and that he knew where the remote was but not the TV. On the Mount Ebal material, he said the proposed readings looked like a random jumble rather than real epigraphy, compared the work to pareidolia, and argued the responsible conclusion should have been that no legible inscription was present. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=2210" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:50</a> | What if an old pharaoh could not finish the Sed festival? | He said there is no record of a pharaoh failing it, no time limit was attached, and physical or mental incapacity could lead to proxy rule or a `Renaissance` arrangement. | He explained the Sed festival as a post-thirty-year ritual and obstacle-course display of the king's continued fitness. Since no time limit is attested, an elderly king could take as long as needed, but if incapacity made rule impossible, especially mental incapacity, duties could shift to a vizier or proxy under a rebirth or Renaissance arrangement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=2667" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:27</a> | How would he respond to the claim that Exodus 21:2-6, especially verse 4, is immoral? | He said the law regulates an already immoral cultural situation and tries to protect vulnerable women within that setting rather than endorsing the culture itself. | He treated the passage as a regulation of Hebrew debt slavery inside a wider ancient Near Eastern culture that already accepted slavery and treated enslaved women as sexually compromised. In his reading, the law does not approve that culture but tries to curb harm, especially by preventing women and children from being discarded into a setting where remarriage or support would be culturally difficult. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=3427" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:07</a> | Why does God seem to sanction revenge killing Saul's descendants in 2 Samuel 21? | He argued that Saul broke a treaty ratified before God, likely triggering covenant sanctions, so the famine and deaths relate to repairing that broken covenant. | He read the episode through ancient treaty logic: Israel had sworn a covenant with the Gibeonites before Yahweh, and Saul's attack dishonored that divine guarantee. The famine therefore reflects covenant curse language, and the requested deaths likely correspond to treaty sanctions rather than private revenge, even though the Gibeonites themselves are portrayed as reluctant to pursue money or arbitrary killing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=3938" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:38</a> | What archaeological discovery would be hardest to reconcile with the biblical narrative? | He said the Mount Ebal amulet would be a major problem if the claims were true, but he does not think the claimed reading and dating are legitimate. | He said the Mount Ebal claims would create a major chronological problem if taken at face value because they would place Exodus-related material far earlier than the cultural context presented in Exodus. He immediately qualified that he does not regard the claimed reading or dating as legitimate, so the difficulty is hypothetical rather than an actual challenge. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=4109" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:29</a> | Which would be the bigger problem, the Mount Ebal amulet or the Jerusalem curse tablet? | He said the amulet would be bigger if its reading and dating were correct; a Jerusalem curse tablet would fit an Amorite city around 1400 BC, though he rejected both epigraphies. | He distinguished the discoveries from the interpretations: the artifacts may exist, but he called the proposed epigraphies bogus. The Ebal amulet would be more disruptive if its reading and dating were right, while an Amorite inscription at Jerusalem around 1400 BC would be historically unsurprising and therefore less problematic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=4189" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:49</a> | What are some strong popular-level books on Old Testament reliability? | He recommended titles such as James Hoffmeier on archaeology, Kenneth Kitchen on Old Testament reliability, Peter J. Williams on the gospels, and work by John Walton for context. | After the question was clarified as popular-level, he named Hoffmeier's archaeology book as useful but not deep, Kitchen's Old Testament reliability work as a classic on the popular-academic boundary, and Peter J. Williams on the Gospels as worth having despite being New Testament focused. He also said John Walton's contextual work helps many reliability questions by explaining the ancient setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=4383" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:03</a> | Is it true that scholars once believed ancient Israel was a myth? | He said some Enlightenment-era skeptics did, especially following Voltaire, but archaeology has forced many such claims about Israel, Hittites, Belshazzar, and others to be walked back. | He traced the claim especially to Enlightenment skepticism and Voltaire's argument that biblical cities had not been found, while noting that even then Jerusalem and Rome were obvious complications. He said nineteenth-century archaeology changed the discussion, and later discoveries forced earlier dismissals of Israel, the Hittites, Belshazzar, David, and related peoples or figures to be moderated or abandoned. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=4898" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:38</a> | Was the first catapult in the world invented in Judah? | He said it is possible but uncertain because the relevant biblical device may only have dropped stones rather than used stored mechanical energy like a true catapult. | He explained that catapults are hard to identify archaeologically because their materials are often perishable and because siege devices overlap in function. The biblical reference may describe a mechanism that released stones from a wall, but unless it stored and converted energy to hurl stones beyond gravity, he would not call it a true catapult. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=5074" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:34</a> | What is the Late Bronze Age context of Genesis 6:4 compared with a Second Temple Judaism context? | He contrasted Heiser's Second Temple reading with a Bronze Age priestly-vow context, taking Nephilim as offspring connected to broken vows rather than demigod beings. | He summarized Heiser's Second Temple view as angelic beings producing cursed demigod-like Nephilim. He then argued that Late Bronze Age parallels point instead to priestly language, with "sons" and "daughters" of deities functioning as priests and priestesses, and connected the Nephilim to offspring from broken celibacy vows whose consequences were understood as socially disastrous. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=5545" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:25</a> | How plausible is it to deny the historicity of Jesus, Paul, or even early Christians altogether? | He said the apostolic fathers and their teacher-student chain make that skepticism very hard to sustain. | He argued that the apostolic fathers create an early and continuous chain of testimony from students to teachers back toward the apostles and Jesus. Figures such as Polycarp and Clement of Rome, in his view, make it difficult to deny the existence of the apostles or the early Christian movement without rejecting a substantial causal chain of historical evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=5683" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:43</a> | Does anyone doubt Socrates lived? | He said no, even though the evidence mainly comes through Plato and Xenophon, and contrasted that with skeptical claims about Christian figures. | He said Socrates is generally accepted even though the core evidence comes through Plato and Xenophon, both students of Socrates. He used that comparison to argue that skepticism about Jesus or Paul often exceeds the standards applied to other ancient figures, even when scholars may still debate embellishment or interpretation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=5943" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:03</a> | Did ancient Egyptian women have equal rights to men of the same social class? | He said Egyptian women had unusually broad rights for the ancient Near East, including divorce, property, adoption, and economic options, with exceptions by status. | He said Egyptian women, except in constrained statuses such as slaves, insanity, or royal daughters, had broader legal and economic agency than women in many neighboring cultures. He described rights to initiate divorce, retain property shares, work, serve in temples, adopt children, and sometimes use legal loopholes around slavery and remarriage, while noting inheritance was not absolute. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=6407" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:47</a> | Are there good books on disturbing Old Testament passages besides Paul Copan? | He said Copan fills an important niche, older broad works such as Gleason Archer's and the McDowells' attempts have problems, and slavery-law literature is more specialized. | He said slavery law is a specialized field with a large literature, but he did not name many authors because it is not his focus. For broad difficult-passage treatments, he said Copan fills a hard niche; older attempts such as Gleason Archer's broad work and the McDowells' kitchen-sink approach are less useful because they either give weak single answers or list options without enough evaluation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=7074" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:54</a> | What about the claim that Old Testament sacrifices purified holy spaces while Jesus forgives sins, and that New Testament authors confused this? | He called it partly true but partly mistaken: purification sacrifices and sin forgiveness are distinct categories, yet Jesus' sacrifice also covers the broader sacrificial system. | He said Old Testament sacrifices cover several categories, including sin, purification, vows, and thanksgiving, so purification and forgiveness should not be collapsed into the same category. At the same time, he said Jesus' final sacrifice relates to the whole sacrificial system, so the quote was half right in keeping categories distinct but wrong if it excluded Jesus' sacrifice from covering those other sacrificial functions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=7495" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:55</a> | Did Tertullian believe the Spirit and Son were created first but temporary and would one day cease? | He said he would need the exact source and context because Tertullian is often misquoted, especially when he is quoting and then refuting modalist opponents. | He refused to endorse the claim without a precise citation because Tertullian is often quoted from sections where he is presenting an opponent's position before refuting it. He specifically warned that modalist material associated with Noetus can be misattributed to Tertullian if readers do not follow the longer argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=7892" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:32</a> | What are his thoughts on Total War: Pharaoh? | He had not seen or played it yet, but said the described setting sounded like late Dynasty 19 or early Dynasty 20 and welcomed more Egypt-themed games. | He said he had not watched trailers, ordered the game, or played it, so he could not review it. Based on the description, he guessed it might be set around the end of Dynasty 19 or start of Dynasty 20, with places such as Pi-Ramesses, Memphis, or Thebes potentially relevant, and he welcomed games that bring ancient Egypt back into popular culture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=8256" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:36</a> | What can be used instead of Strong's Concordance? | He recommended HALOT for Hebrew and Aramaic, BDAG for New Testament Greek, and Holladay's concise Hebrew-Aramaic lexicon as a cheaper Old Testament option. | He strongly criticized Strong's as outdated and inadequate for serious lexical work because it does not properly handle historical language change. He recommended HALOT for Hebrew and Aramaic, BDAG for Greek, and Holladay's concise Hebrew and Aramaic lexicon as a less expensive but still better Old Testament option. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=8790" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:30</a> | Who is the naked young man in Mark 14:51-52? | He said the text does not identify him and leaned toward a literary Gestalt for the fleeing disciples, while allowing that he could have been a real person. | He emphasized that Mark does not name the young man, and that identifying him with Mark is unlikely because Mark was probably not among the original disciples. He leaned toward a literary device in which the shameful flight of the disciples is compressed into one figure, while allowing that an unusual real event remains possible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=9160" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:40</a> | What is his view of Michael Heiser's argument that the tribes under the conquest ban were Nephilim tribes? | He said the spy report is exaggerated, Heiser conflates Nephilim and Rephaim, and the biblical text does not require that identification even if Second Temple literature supports it. | He argued that Numbers 13 is explicitly a bad and exaggerated spy report, so its giant language should not be pressed flatly. He also distinguished Rephaim as very tall humans from Nephilim, saying Heiser's conflation is supported by later Second Temple traditions but not required by the biblical text itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=10157" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:17</a> | How does Christianity influence David and Kiara's lives? | He said it led him to give up a high-paying career, pursue ministry and education work, and see God's provision through difficult turns. | Speaking mainly for himself, he said Christianity shaped his whole vocation, including leaving a six-figure career, pursuing seminary and doctoral work, and dedicating himself to educating the church. He described selling possessions, living through financial uncertainty, starting the channel unexpectedly during the pandemic, and seeing God's provision and guidance through both scarcity and opportunity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=10730" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:50</a> | What are his thoughts on Builders of Egypt? | He said he did not know whether it was a game or video series and could not comment because he had not seen or played it. | He did not know what Builders of Egypt was and explicitly declined to give an opinion. If it was a game or video series, he said he had neither seen nor played it, so he could not comment responsibly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=10804" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:04</a> | What did he think of Rob's Mount Ebal short? | He said it was well done and funny, but the image of Scott Stripling dancing was burned into his memory and he wished he had not seen it. | He answered mostly humorously, saying the short was well done and funny but that one visual gag had lodged unpleasantly in his memory. The substantive judgment was positive on execution, with the caveat that he wished he could unsee the dancing image. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=10923" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:03</a> | Does Heiser's view provide a strong apologetic for conquest allegations compared with human-Nephilim views? | He said no; even if Heiser were right, it would not solve the genocide objection, and genre and lexical issues provide better explanations. | He said Heiser's view would not answer the moral objection if the claim is still that Israel wiped out whole peoples, even if those peoples were defined as Nephilim. He preferred explanations based on ancient Near Eastern warfare genre, hyperbolic language, and lexical issues around the ban, and said those make the Nephilim apologetic unnecessary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=11381" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:41</a> | What were the most distant regions or peoples ancient Egyptians had contact with in Europe, Asia, and Africa? | He said Egyptian trade contacts changed by period but reached Punt/Ethiopia, Cyprus and the Greek islands, Anatolia, Spain's Atlantic coast, India, and by Dynasty 20 possibly China. | He emphasized that the answer depends on period because Egypt's trade networks expanded over time. He cited Punt or Ethiopia to the south, Cyprus, the Greek islands, and Anatolia to the north, scarabs reaching Spain's Atlantic coast to the west, goods from Afghanistan and India in Dynasty 18 contexts, and possible silk from China by Dynasty 20. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=11777" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:17</a> | Is the idea that demons are spirits of dead Nephilim unbiblical? | He said it is not directly contradicted but is unsupported or non-biblical, and the evidence leans against it. | He distinguished "unbiblical" from "non-biblical": the Bible may not directly contradict the idea, but he does not think it supports it. He said the evidence leans against demons as dead Nephilim, in part because he reads demonic beings as existing from the beginning of creation and rejects the Second Temple framing behind half-demonic Nephilim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=12030" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:30</a> | Could Jude and Enoch be quoting a shared tradition rather than Jude quoting Enoch, and could the prophecy be separate from the rest of Enoch? | He said either Jude quoting Enoch or both drawing from a shared tradition is possible, but the question remains speculative and hard to prove. | He said a shared tradition or predecessor document is possible, and it is also possible that Jude quotes Enoch directly because of the verbal similarities. He did not take a firm position because hypothetical source relationships are difficult to prove and can move quickly from evidence into speculation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=12264" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:24</a> | Are there relations between Israelite timekeeping and the calendars of Canaanites or Egyptians? | He said Israel's calendar began as lunar, adopted features from neighboring systems over time, used regnal dating under Egyptian influence, and later absorbed Mesopotamian intercalation. | He described Israel's early calendar as lunar and similar in some ways to regional systems, with months divided into weeks and regnal dating affected by whoever held hegemony. He said Israelite practice shifted over time, later incorporating Mesopotamian intercalation, while Egyptian calendars themselves could drift because they lacked intercalation until much later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=12683" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:23</a> | Could Goliath have suffered from the same pituitary-gland problem as Robert Wadlow? | He doubted it because Goliath and related giants are portrayed as accomplished warriors, unlike most modern pituitary-gigantism cases, but said skeletons would be needed to know. | He said most modern pituitary-gigantism cases involve serious health and mobility issues, whereas Goliath and related figures are portrayed as capable warriors. He therefore doubted the same medical explanation, but he also said the question cannot be settled without skeletal evidence showing how such ancient giantism manifested. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=12896" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:56</a> | Does 2 John still apply if heretical missionaries such as Mormons or Jehovah's Witnesses come to a home? | He said the passage concerns giving false teachers a platform in a house church, not ordinary hospitality or evangelistic conversation, so the application depends on whether they are being allowed to teach. | He argued that "receiving" in 2 John is about admitting false teachers into the house church or giving them a recognized teaching platform, not about letting a plumber, unbelieving guest, or missionary into the house for conversation. Hospitality and evangelistic discussion can be appropriate, but hosting their Bible study or granting them authority to teach would violate the passage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=13415" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:43:35</a> | Should Christians still celebrate the Sabbath? | He said Christians are not under the Sinai covenant requirement, but taking a Sabbath rest can still be healthy and beneficial. | He said the Sabbath functioned as a sign of the Sinai covenant and that Christians are under the new covenant rather than the Sinai covenant. Still, he described Sabbath rest as healthy, flexible in day, and intended for human benefit, not as a legalistic burden. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=13622" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:02</a> | How are ancient dates dated when dates can be unreliable, such as for the late Exodus period? | He said chronologists use king lists and synchronisms such as battles, letters, marriages, and royal gifts to align rulers and rule out impossible date schemes. | He explained that some dates can be hard pegged by multiple sources, while earlier periods rely on king lists and synchronisms. A synchronism is evidence that two rulers or events overlap, such as diplomatic letters, battles, marriages, commemorative scarabs, or royal gifts, and enough of these can rule out chronologies that put rulers in impossible relationships. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=14144" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:44</a> | How serious was the insubordination of the Egyptian midwives in Exodus 1? | He said Pharaoh's order violated Egyptian justice traditions, so their refusal was probably not treated as severe, though they likely still faced brutal questioning or beating. | He said Egypt lacked written law for much of its history but had a strong tradition of justice, and Pharaoh's order to murder infants violated that tradition. The midwives' excuse was probably not believed, but because refusing an unjust order was different from ordinary rebellion, he thought the likely consequence was harsh interrogation and beating rather than the most severe punishment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/y5KgOW9yQ3w?t=14635" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:03:55</a> | In Egyptian naming magic, what was a person's real name, and did Egyptians understand the arbitrariness of names? | He said Egyptians distinguished a public name from a hidden true name that functioned like a magical key to control a being. | He said Egyptians understood ordinary names could be arbitrary, but also believed gods, humans, and spirits had hidden true names. In magic, the true name disclosed something controlling about the being, so a curse or spell needed that hidden name rather than merely the public name; discovering and protecting such names was therefore part of magical practice. |
